claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to know a bit about your child (or children):

**How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **20 or over** don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please let me know your child's age, and if you have multiple children, we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have two children actually - Alex, who's 13, and Blake, who's 15. They're both under 16, so I hope that means things should be straightforward?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. Yes, having children under 16 is a good start! Let me check the requirements for Alex (13) and Blake (15).

Since both Alex and Blake are under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. However, there are still a few important checks we need to go through.

Let me navigate through the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to ask:

**Are Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This check applies to children aged 16 and over, but I need to confirm for Blake who is 15. Generally, children under 16 in the UK have restrictions on working hours anyway, but I need to verify that neither child is working 24 hours or more per week.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work that much at all. Alex doesn't have a job yet - too young really. Blake does a Saturday shift at a local shop, but it's just pocket money, nothing like 24 hours a week!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's perfectly fine. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, I need to confirm that neither child is receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits in their own name

Given their ages (13 and 15) and that they're just doing part-time/no work, I expect the answer is that they're not receiving these benefits, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - neither of them get any benefits in their own names. They're just kids really, still at school and living at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue through the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check:

**Are Alex or Blake in any type of apprenticeship?**

This is relevant because:
- Most apprenticeships (in England and Scotland) would disqualify them from Child Benefit
- Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales are still approved
- Given they're 13 and 15 and still at school, I expect they're not in apprenticeships, but I need to confirm

Are either of them in an apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing apprenticeships. They're both still at school - just regular secondary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's very straightforward then. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status as a student with sufficient resources
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK?** Or does one of the other situations apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester. Always have been, so nothing complicated there!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's straightforward - you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You meet this requirement if ANY of the following applies:

1. **You live with the children** (this is the most common situation)
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

From what you've mentioned (they're living at home with you), it sounds like you live with them. Can you confirm that Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives with me at home, yes. But Blake actually lives with my ex-partner. I do help out financially though - I contribute towards Blake's upkeep, buying clothes and helping with school things and that sort of stuff.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - it's important to understand each child's situation separately.

So to confirm:
- **Alex (13)** lives with you at home ✓
- **Blake (15)** lives with your ex-partner, but you contribute financially to Blake's upkeep

For Blake, the contributions you make (buying clothes, helping with school things, etc.) can count as being "responsible" for the child if they equal or exceed the amount of Child Benefit. 

Let me check the current Child Benefit rates for you:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see the rates aren't specified in my reference data, but currently Child Benefit is:
- £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child
- £16.95 per week for additional children

For Blake, you'd need to be contributing at least the equivalent of Child Benefit (which would be £16.95 per week or about £73-74 per month as the second child) through your contributions like clothes, school supplies, etc.

**Do your contributions towards Blake's upkeep (clothes, school things, etc.) amount to at least around £74 per month or £17 per week?**

This is important because if both you and your ex-partner are responsible for Blake, we'll need to check who should claim (only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'd say so. I probably spend a bit more than that actually - between clothes, school uniform, shoes, dinners, bits and pieces... it adds up! I'd say I'm contributing about £26 a week towards Blake's needs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That definitely meets the responsibility requirement for Blake (you're contributing more than the Child Benefit amount). So you are responsible for both children:

- **Alex (13)** - you live with Alex ✓
- **Blake (15)** - you contribute £26/week towards Blake's upkeep ✓

Now, this brings up an important question. Let me continue with the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is a crucial question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

Specifically, since Blake lives with your ex-partner, is your ex-partner claiming Child Benefit for Blake? And is anyone else claiming for Alex?

This matters because:
- Only ONE person can claim Child Benefit for each child
- If your ex-partner is already claiming for Blake, you'd need to agree on who should claim
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the decision

Can you tell me:
1. Is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
2. Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Blake - we agreed that I would handle all that since I'm contributing financially anyway. And no one else is claiming for Alex either. It's just me looking to claim for both of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's very clear - no one else is claiming for either child, and you and your ex-partner have agreed that you'll be the one to claim. Let me continue with the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check the children's living arrangements:

**Are Alex or Blake in any of these situations?**

- In hospital (as an in-patient)
- In local authority care
- Being fostered
- Or are they living normally (Alex with you, Blake with your ex-partner)?

From what you've told me, it sounds like they're both living normally - Alex at home with you and Blake with your ex-partner. Can you confirm neither of them is currently in hospital or in care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally with me, yes. But Blake is actually in local authority care - he's been there for a couple of months now. He does come home to stay with me on the weekends though, so I still see him regularly.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - this is an important detail. Blake being in local authority care changes things, and the fact that he comes home on weekends is relevant too.

Let me check the specific rules for children in care:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is important. For Blake who is in local authority care, you can still claim Child Benefit if either:

1. **Blake has been in care for 8 weeks or less**, OR
2. **Blake spends at least 24 hours per week at home with you**

You mentioned Blake has been in care for "a couple of months" and comes home on weekends. Let me ask:

**For Blake:**
- Exactly how long has Blake been in local authority care? (Is it more or less than 8 weeks?)
- When Blake comes home on weekends, does he spend at least 24 hours per week at home with you? (For example, does he come Friday evening and stay until Sunday, or similar?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in care for about 4 weeks now, so still under the 8-week limit. And yes, when he comes home on the weekends, he's definitely with me for more than 24 hours - usually Friday after school until Sunday evening, so that's easily a full day or more.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's really helpful information. Blake meets the care requirement because:
- He's been in care for only 4 weeks (under the 8-week limit) ✓
- He spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you ✓

So you can still claim Child Benefit for Blake. Let me continue with the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask one final important question:

**Is the local council paying towards either Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

For Blake specifically, since he's in local authority care - is the council paying for his accommodation or maintenance costs while he's in care?

This is the final check before I can give you a determination on your eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying for Alex - he lives with me and I cover everything for him. As for Blake, the council isn't giving me anything for his maintenance while he's in care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I need to clarify this carefully because it's a crucial point for Blake's eligibility.

When Blake is in local authority care during the week, is the **local council** paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance costs **while he's in care** (not paying you, but paying for his care placement)?

Usually when a child is in local authority care, the council is paying for their accommodation and upkeep during the time they're in the care placement, even if they're coming home on weekends.

Can you clarify:
- Is the council paying for Blake's care placement costs (his accommodation and maintenance while he's in the care facility)?

This is different from whether they're paying *you* - I'm asking whether the council is covering the costs of Blake being in care.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I see what you mean. Yes, the council is paying for Blake's care placement during the week - they're covering the costs of him being at the care home. I thought you were asking if they were paying me directly, but yes, they are paying for his accommodation while he's there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that - it's an important distinction. This does affect Blake's eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me navigate to the outcome:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I see. Now let me check Alex's situation. Since Alex lives with you and the council isn't paying for Alex, let me trace through Alex's eligibility separately. Let me start a fresh assessment path for Alex:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now let me check what the ELIGIBLE outcome says:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. Now let me provide you with the complete assessment for both children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

I've completed the assessment for both of your children. Here's what I found:

---

### **Alex (age 13): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. 

Alex meets all the qualifying criteria:
- ✅ Under 16 years old (automatically qualifies)
- ✅ Not working 24+ hours per week
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ Attending regular secondary school (not in a disqualifying apprenticeship)
- ✅ You meet UK residency requirements (living in Manchester)
- ✅ You are responsible for Alex (lives with you)
- ✅ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✅ Living normally at home with you
- ✅ Council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

---

### **Blake (age 15): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

While Blake meets most of the basic criteria (age, not working full-time, etc.), there is a **critical disqualifying factor**: Blake is in local authority care and the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance at the care placement.

**The rule states:** You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

I know this is difficult, especially since:
- You're contributing financially to Blake's upkeep (£26/week)
- Blake comes home on weekends and spends 24+ hours with you
- Blake has only been in care for 4 weeks

However, the fact that the council is funding the care placement means Child Benefit cannot be claimed for Blake while this arrangement continues.

---

### **Next Steps:**

**For Alex:**
1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - you can do this online or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earn over £60,000, you may need to pay some of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** will be made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

**For Blake:**
- If Blake's care situation changes (e.g., if the council stops paying for accommodation, or if Blake returns home permanently), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for Blake at that point
- It may be worth contacting HMRC or Citizens Advice if Blake's care arrangement changes

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about your eligibility or the application process for Alex?

reasonings: Alex (age 13) is eligible for Child Benefit. Alex meets all the qualifying criteria: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies by age; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - attending regular secondary school; (5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK (Manchester) - meets residency requirement; (6) You are responsible for Alex - Alex lives with you at home; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home with you; (9) The local council is NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all eligibility requirements.

Blake (age 15) is NOT eligible for Child Benefit. While Blake meets most qualifying criteria as a child under 16, there is a critical disqualifying factor: Blake is in local authority care and the local council IS paying towards Blake's accommodation and maintenance costs at the care placement. According to the eligibility rules, you generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Although Blake: (1) is under 16; (2) has been in care for only 4 weeks (under 8 weeks); (3) spends 24+ hours per week at home with you on weekends; and (4) you contribute financially to Blake's upkeep - the fact that the council is paying for the care placement accommodation means Blake does not qualify for Child Benefit to be claimed by you.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (13) and Blake (15). The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives at home and meets all qualifying criteria. However, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because Blake is in local authority care and the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance at the care placement, which is a disqualifying factor under Child Benefit rules.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?

Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 13) is eligible for Child Benefit. Alex meets all the qualifying criteria: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies by age; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - attending regular secondary school; (5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK (Manchester) - meets residency requirement; (6) You are responsible for Alex - Alex lives with you at home; (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home with you; (9) The local council is NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all eligibility requirements.

Blake (age 15) is NOT eligible for Child Benefit. While Blake meets most qualifying criteria as a child under 16, there is a critical disqualifying factor: Blake is in local authority care and the local council IS paying towards Blake's accommodation and maintenance costs at the care placement. According to the eligibility rules, you generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance. Although Blake: (1) is under 16; (2) has been in care for only 4 weeks (under 8 weeks); (3) spends 24+ hours per week at home with you on weekends; and (4) you contribute financially to Blake's upkeep - the fact that the council is paying for the care placement accommodation means Blake does not qualify for Child Benefit to be claimed by you.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (13) and Blake (15). The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives at home and meets all qualifying criteria. However, the claimant cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because Blake is in local authority care and the council is paying for Blake's accommodation and maintenance at the care placement, which is a disqualifying factor under Child Benefit rules.
